---
title: "Predicting College Major Field of Study: A Neural Network Approach Using the Education Longitudinal Study of 2002"
shorttitle: "Predicting College Major with Neural Networks"
author:
  - name: "Author Name"
    affiliation: "Institution"
    email: "author@institution.edu"
abstract: |
  Using data from the Education Longitudinal Study of 2002 (ELS:2002), this
  study applies a feedforward neural network to predict bachelor's degree
  major field of study (2-digit CIP code) from student background
  characteristics, psychosocial scales, high school coursetaking transcripts,
  and early postsecondary experiences measured at or before the 2006 second
  follow-up. The model is implemented in the tidymodels framework using a
  keras engine and trained on [N] students who ever enrolled in postsecondary
  education and subsequently completed a bachelor's degree. Hyperparameters
  are selected via Latin hypercube grid search across five-fold
  cross-validation. Results indicate [summary of findings]. Variable
  importance analysis reveals that [key predictors] are most strongly
  associated with major field, with implications for [theoretical and
  practical contributions].
keywords: "college major, neural networks, ELS:2002, machine learning, higher education"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  stevetemplates::article3:
    citation_package: biblatex
bibliography: references.bib
biblio-style: apa
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{longtable}
  - \usepackage{array}
  - \usepackage{multirow}
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \floatplacement{figure}{H}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.pos = "H",
  out.width = "100%"
)

library(targets)
library(tidyverse)
library(kableExtra)
library(here)

# Load all targets needed for this paper
# No computation happens here — only tar_read() / tar_load() calls
tar_load(c(
  analytic_sample,
  performance_metrics,
  confusion_matrix,
  per_class_metrics,
  vip_scores,
  tbl_sample_descriptives,
  tbl_performance,
  cip_labels,
  best_params
))
```

# Introduction

Which college major a student chooses is among the most consequential
educational decisions of early adulthood, with lasting implications for
occupational attainment, earnings trajectories, and social mobility
[@ma2016; @arcidiacono2004]. Yet the factors that channel students into
particular fields of study remain only partially understood. Prior research
has documented the role of socioeconomic background [@mullen2010],
prior academic achievement [@trusty2002], gender [@charles2004], and
educational expectations [@crisp2009], but these studies have largely relied
on linear models that may miss complex, non-linear interactions among
predictors.

This paper applies a feedforward neural network to predict bachelor's degree
major field of study — coded at the 2-digit Classification of Instructional
Programs (CIP) level — using data from the Education Longitudinal Study of
2002 (ELS:2002). ELS:2002 provides an unusually rich set of predictors
spanning cognitive achievement, psychosocial attitudes and motivations, high
school coursetaking trajectories, and early postsecondary experiences, all
measured before or concurrent with the 2006 second follow-up, well prior to
the 2012 degree outcome. The neural network is trained within the tidymodels
framework [@kuhn2022], enabling reproducible integration of preprocessing,
hyperparameter tuning, and model evaluation.

We make three contributions. First, [contribution 1]. Second,
[contribution 2]. Third, [contribution 3].

# Data

## Education Longitudinal Study of 2002

The Education Longitudinal Study of 2002 is a nationally representative
longitudinal study of U.S. tenth graders administered by the National Center
for Education Statistics [@ingels2014]. The study followed respondents
through a base year (2002), first follow-up (2004), second follow-up (2006),
and third follow-up (2012), supplemented by high school and postsecondary
transcripts.

## Analytic Sample

```{r sample-size}
n_total <- nrow(analytic_sample)
```

The analytic sample is restricted to respondents who (1) ever enrolled in a
postsecondary institution by the 2006 second follow-up and (2) have an
observed bachelor's degree major field recorded in the 2013 postsecondary
transcript data. This yields an analytic sample of *N* = `r scales::comma(n_total)`.

## Outcome Variable

The outcome is the 2-digit CIP code of the student's first known bachelor's
degree (`F3TZBCH1CIP2`), as recorded in the ELS:2002 postsecondary transcript
file. [Describe CIP collapse decisions and distribution here; pull from
`tar_read(cip_labels)` as needed.]

Figure \ref{fig:outcome-dist} shows the distribution of major fields in the
analytic sample.

```{r fig-outcome-dist, fig.cap="Distribution of bachelor's degree major fields in the analytic sample."}
knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "fig1_outcome_dist.png"))
```

## Predictor Variables

Predictors are drawn from four waves of ELS:2002 data, all measured at or
before the second follow-up in 2006. Table \ref{tab:variable-list} in the
online appendix provides a complete variable list with sources, waves, and
coding. Here we briefly describe each domain.

**Cognitive achievement.** Math and reading standardized T scores from the
ELS base year assessment (`BYTXMSTD`, `BYTXRSTD`), scaled to a national mean
of 50 and standard deviation of 10 [@ingels2014].

**Psychosocial scales.** Six composite scales from the base year student
questionnaire: math self-efficacy, English self-efficacy, control expectation
(locus of control), instrumental motivation, effort and persistence, and
class preparation [@ingels2014]. A school academic climate scale from the
administrator questionnaire is also included.

**Aspirations and plans.** Student and parent educational expectations,
expected occupations at age 30 and immediately after high school, and
postsecondary plans from both the base year and first follow-up.

**High school coursetaking.** Carnegie unit counts by subject area,
AP/IB course counts by subject, cumulative GPA, and summary pipeline
indicators (highest math course, math and science coursetaking pipelines,
curriculum concentration) from the restricted-use high school transcript
file.

**Postsecondary context.** Institutional characteristics of the first
attended postsecondary institution, enrollment intensity, financial aid
receipt, remedial coursetaking, and early postsecondary GPA and credit
accumulation from the second follow-up and postsecondary transcript data.

## Missing Data

Missing predictor values were imputed within the tidymodels preprocessing
recipe using median imputation for numeric variables and mode imputation for
nominal variables, with imputation statistics estimated from training data
only to prevent data leakage. Missingness indicator variables were added for
postsecondary transcript GPA and credits earned in years one and two, as the
fact of missing transcript data is itself informative about enrollment
pathways. Figure A1 in the online appendix shows missingness rates by
variable.

# Methods

## Neural Network Architecture

We estimated a feedforward neural network with two fully connected hidden
layers using the `keras` engine in the tidymodels `parsnip` package
[@falbel2022]. The network takes a normalized, one-hot encoded feature matrix
as input and produces a softmax probability distribution over major field
categories as output. Hidden layers use rectified linear unit (ReLU)
activation. The model is trained with the Adam optimizer and categorical
cross-entropy loss.

## Preprocessing

All preprocessing was implemented in a tidymodels recipe applied to training
data only, with transformations carried forward to the test set and
cross-validation folds to prevent leakage. Steps included: median and mode
imputation, one-hot encoding of nominal predictors, removal of zero-variance
columns, and standardization of all numeric predictors to mean zero and unit
variance.

## Hyperparameter Tuning

Five hyperparameters were tuned via a 20-point Latin hypercube grid search
across five-fold cross-validation stratified on major field: hidden units per
layer (range: 32–256), training epochs (50–300), L2 penalty (10^-5^ to
10^-1^), dropout rate (0–0.5), and learning rate (10^-4^ to 10^-2^). The
primary tuning metric was macro-weighted one-versus-rest ROC AUC. The optimal
configuration is reported in Table \ref{tab:best-params}.

```{r tbl-best-params}
best_params %>%
  dplyr::select(hidden_units, epochs, penalty, dropout, learn_rate) %>%
  kableExtra::kbl(
    caption = "Selected hyperparameter configuration.",
    booktabs = TRUE,
    digits = 4,
    col.names = c("Hidden Units", "Epochs", "L2 Penalty",
                  "Dropout", "Learn Rate")
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position"))
```

## Evaluation

The final model was fit on the full training set using the selected
hyperparameters and evaluated on a held-out test set comprising 20% of the
analytic sample. Performance metrics include overall accuracy,
macro-weighted ROC AUC, and mean log loss. Per-class precision, recall, and
F1 are reported in the online appendix.

## Variable Importance

Variable importance was estimated via permutation: each predictor was
randomly shuffled in the test set and the resulting drop in macro-weighted
AUC was recorded, averaged across five permutations. Larger drops indicate
greater model reliance on that feature [@fisher2019].

# Results

## Overall Model Performance

```{r performance-inline}
acc   <- performance_metrics %>% dplyr::filter(.metric == "accuracy") %>% dplyr::pull(.estimate)
auc   <- performance_metrics %>% dplyr::filter(.metric == "roc_auc")  %>% dplyr::pull(.estimate)
loss  <- performance_metrics %>% dplyr::filter(.metric == "mn_log_loss") %>% dplyr::pull(.estimate)
```

The final model achieved an overall accuracy of `r scales::percent(acc, accuracy = 0.1)`,
a macro-weighted ROC AUC of `r round(auc, 3)`, and a mean log loss of
`r round(loss, 3)` on the held-out test set. Figure \ref{fig:confusion-matrix}
shows the confusion matrix across major field categories.

```{r fig-confusion-matrix, fig.cap="Confusion matrix of predicted versus true major fields on the held-out test set."}
knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "fig3_confusion_matrix.png"))
```

## Variable Importance

Figure \ref{fig:vip} displays the 30 most important predictors by permutation
importance. [Narrative description of top predictors and what they reveal
about major field determination.]

```{r fig-vip, fig.cap="Permutation-based variable importance: top 30 predictors by mean decrease in macro-weighted AUC."}
knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "fig4_vip.png"))
```

# Discussion

[Discussion of main findings, theoretical implications, connections to prior
literature on major field choice, and practical implications.]

## Limitations

Several limitations should be noted. First, the analytic sample is restricted
to students who completed a bachelor's degree, introducing selection bias
that limits generalizability to all college entrants. Second, [limitation 2].
Third, permutation importance scores reflect model reliance on each feature,
not causal effects; correlated predictors will have their importance
underestimated [@molnar2020].

# Conclusion

[Summary of contributions and future directions.]

# References {-}

\setlength{\parindent}{-0.2in}
\setlength{\leftskip}{0.2in}
\noindent
